root: ^Satan stands on the outer shell of the universe.

^Below him — impossibly far, impossibly near — the created world turns within its crystalline spheres. Above, the stars blaze in their fixed courses. Around him, the cold silence of space, unbroken by wind or breath or any living sound.
^He has passed through Chaos. He has found the edge of God's creation. And now he stands upon it like a thief upon a rooftop, looking for a way in.

^The shell beneath his feet is hard and smooth — the outermost boundary of the cosmos. Beyond it, Chaos still churns. Within it, order reigns. Somewhere inside, hidden among the spheres within spheres, lies Earth. And on Earth, the garden. And in the garden...
^But first he must find a way through.

^Satan circles the great sphere, searching. His wings — mended somewhat since the passage through Chaos — carry him over vast expanses of featureless shell. The stars wheel below him like distant lamps.
^Search methodically. There must be an opening — God's creation is not yet fully sealed.
^Follow the light. Where Heaven's radiance falls strongest, there will be a passage.
^Pause and observe. Watch how the angels come and go — they must have a route.

c-0: ^Satan flies with purpose, tracing the curve of creation. His eyes — sharper than any mortal's, though dimmed since the fall — scan the surface for any crack, any seam, any imperfection in God's handiwork.
^And there — where the light of a distant star pools in a shallow depression — he finds it. A stairway. Golden, gleaming, descending through the shell into the spheres below.
^Jacob's Ladder. The path by which angels descend from Heaven to Earth and return.
c-1: ^Satan turns toward the brightest point on the shell — a place where Heaven's light pours down like a waterfall of gold. The radiance is almost painful to his darkened eyes, but he endures it.
^Where light falls, doors open. It has always been so.
^And there it is: a stairway of golden light, descending through the shell of the universe, down through the spheres, toward Earth. The path the angels use. Unguarded. Unbarred.
c-2: ^Satan waits. He has learned patience in Hell — the long patience of the defeated, which is not resignation but calculation.

^And his patience is rewarded. Far across the shell, a point of light descends — an angel, passing from Heaven to Earth on some errand of grace. Satan marks the spot. He watches the angel vanish through an opening in the shell.
^A stairway. Golden, shining, piercing the boundary between the celestial and the terrestrial. He has his way in.

the_descent: ^Satan alights at the top of the stairway. Below, the spheres of the cosmos unfold — the sphere of fixed stars, then Saturn, Jupiter, Mars, the Sun, Venus, Mercury, the Moon — and at the center of it all, Earth.
^The beauty of it strikes him like a blow.

^He has seen Heaven's splendour. He has built Pandemonium's dark magnificence. But this — this small, perfect, turning world — has a beauty that is neither of those. It is the beauty of something new. Something innocent. Something that has not yet learned to be afraid.
^But he cannot go as himself. An archangel — fallen or not — descending through the spheres would be noticed. The guardians of each sphere, the ruling intelligences, would raise the alarm.

^At the foot of the golden stairway, where it meets the sphere of the Sun, Satan encounters a young angel — a stripling cherub, bright-eyed and eager, gazing at the wonders of creation with undimmed delight.
^Uriel. The angel of the Sun. The sharpest-sighted of all Heaven's host. If anyone would see through a disguise...
^But Satan has not survived this long without cunning.
^Take the form of a lesser angel — a curious cherub on an innocent errand.
^Approach humbly, as a pilgrim who wishes only to admire God's newest work.
^Flatter Uriel. Appeal to his pride as guardian of the Sun's sphere.

c-0: ^Satan reshapes himself. The dark magnificence falls away. The ruined glory, the terrible beauty of the fallen archangel — all hidden beneath the innocent face of a young cherub.

^It is the hardest thing he has done since the fall. Not because the transformation is difficult — angels can take many forms — but because of what it costs his pride. To make himself small. To pretend to be what he once was: bright, innocent, unfallen.
^He approaches Uriel with wide, wondering eyes.
^Noble guardian of the Sun! I am newly created and eager to behold the wonders our Maker has wrought. Can you show me the way to Earth, where the new creatures dwell?
c-1: ^Satan cloaks himself in humility. He takes the form of a wandering angel, travel-worn and reverent, approaching the Sun's sphere with the air of a pilgrim approaching a shrine.
^Bright Uriel, regent of the Sun — I have journeyed far to see the new creation. The fame of Earth has spread through all the spheres. Might you direct a humble traveller to where Man dwells?
c-2: ^Satan becomes a young, admiring angel. His disguised face beams with artless wonder as he approaches Uriel.

^Great Uriel! Of all Heaven's angels, you are said to see furthest and clearest. Surely you know every corner of this vast creation. I wish only to see the world called Earth — to marvel at what God has made. Who better to ask than the sharpest eye in Heaven?

uriel: ^Uriel turns. The angel's face is open, trusting, delighted to meet a fellow spirit who shares his wonder at creation.
^And here is the tragedy: Uriel — the sharpest-sighted spirit in Heaven — sees nothing amiss. Satan's disguise is perfect. The Father of Lies wears innocence like a second skin.

^Of course! Look there — do you see it? That green and gold sphere, third from the Sun? That is Earth. And upon it, in a garden called Eden, the two new creatures walk. They are marvellous — made in God's own image, yet formed of dust.
^Uriel points, and his face shines with genuine joy.
^Take the path that follows the Sun's light. It will lead you to the mount called Niphates, on Earth's eastern edge. From there you can see the garden.
^Thank you, wise Uriel. I will remember your kindness.
^The irony is not lost on him. Kindness. From an angel, to the being who has come to destroy everything that angel loves.
^(Feel the weight of the deception. This is what Satan has become — a creature that weaponises trust.)
^(Uriel is a fool. The sharpest eyes in Heaven, and he cannot see the wolf among the sheep.)
^(Satan deceives because he must. The mission demands it. Pity the necessity, not the act.)

c-0: ^There was a time when Satan did not lie. When his word was as bright and true as his morning light. That being is gone now, replaced by something that can look a trusting angel in the eye and smile while plotting ruin.
^Each deception makes the next one easier. Each mask fits a little more comfortably. And somewhere beneath all the masks, the original face grows harder to remember.
c-1: ^God's sharpest eye, and it cannot pierce a simple glamour. What does that say about Heaven's vaunted vigilance? What does it say about a God who leaves His creation guarded by the gullible?
^Perhaps it says that trust itself is a weakness. And Satan has always known how to exploit weakness.
c-2: ^Satan did not choose to become a deceiver. Defeat made him one. When strength fails, cunning serves. When glory is stripped away, guile remains.
^He deceives Uriel not from malice but from necessity. The angel is an obstacle, and Satan has learned to flow around obstacles like dark water.

the_landing: ^Satan descends. Past the sphere of the Sun, through Venus's warm glow, past Mercury's quicksilver dance, through the Moon's pale circle — and then, at last, the air thickens, the sky turns blue, and he breaks through into the atmosphere of Earth.

^Mount Niphates rises below him. Green. Living. The wind carries scents he has never known — grass, water, earth, growing things. Birds wheel in a sky that is not fire-lit or star-lit but lit by simple, golden sunlight.

^Satan lands on the mountain's peak. His disguise falls away. There is no one here to see, and even deception has its limits. He stands as himself — the ruined archangel, wings scorched, face marked by ages of fury and grief.
^And from the mountaintop, he sees it.

^A garden enclosed by walls of green, threaded with rivers that catch the sunlight. Trees heavy with fruit. Flowers in colours he had forgotten existed. And there, walking among the trees, two figures — tall, beautiful, naked, unashamed.
^Satan stares. And for a long, terrible moment, he does not move.
^(What does Satan feel, looking at Paradise for the first time?)
^(He sees what he has lost — and what God gave freely to creatures of dust.)

c-0: ^Something moves in Satan's chest. Not his heart — he is not sure he still has one. But something. A pressure. A tightness.
^It might be grief. It might be envy. It might be the ghost of a memory — a time when he too walked in light, in a garden that was all gardens, in the presence of a love that asked nothing in return.
^But that was long ago. And what is past cannot be recovered. Only what is ahead can be destroyed.
c-1: ^They have everything. These creatures of clay, these infants of dust — they have been given everything Satan lost. A world. A garden. Freedom. Innocence. The love of their creator, poured out without measure.
^And they did nothing to earn it. They simply... were. Made, and loved, and set in Paradise to tend flowers and name animals while the first and greatest of God's creation burns in Hell.
^The injustice of it is almost too vast to comprehend. Almost.
c-2: ^Satan's eyes narrow. The garden is beautiful, yes. The creatures are innocent, yes. But beauty and innocence are not his concern.

^His concern is the mission. Corrupt Man. Turn God's beloved against Him. Prove that the Almighty's new creation is no better than the old — no more faithful, no more obedient, no more worthy of love.
^The garden is a target. The creatures are tools. And Satan has work to do.

resolve: ^Satan turns his gaze from Eden. He looks up — past the blue sky, past the spheres, toward Heaven, where God sits in His unapproachable light.

^You made them to replace us. You gave them what You took from me. But I will show You that Your precious Man is no more constant than the angels who fell. They will choose as I chose. They will reach for what is forbidden. And when they do...
^He does not finish the thought. He does not need to.
^Satan folds his wings against his back, takes a breath of Eden's sweet air, and begins his descent from Mount Niphates toward the garden.
